1.synthesis: Welcome the one who is weak in faith, but not to quarrel over opinions (Romans 14:1). One person believes he may eat anything; the weak person eats only vegetables. Let not the one who eats despise the one who abstains; let not the one who abstains pass judgment on the one who eats—for God has welcomed him (Romans 14:2–3). Who are you to pass judgment on the servant of another? It is before his own master that he stands or falls—and he will be upheld (Romans 14:4). One person esteems one day as better than another; another esteems all days alike. Each should be fully convinced in his own mind. The one who observes the day observes it in honor of the Lord; the one who eats eats in honor of the Lord, giving thanks (Romans 14:5–6). None of us lives to himself, and none of us dies to himself. If we live, we live to the Lord; if we die, we die to the Lord. So whether we live or die, we are the Lord's (Romans 14:7–8). For to this end Christ died and lived again, that he might be Lord both of the dead and of the living (Romans 14:9). Why do you pass judgment on your brother? Or you, why do you despise your brother? For we will all stand before the judgment seat of God (Romans 14:10–12).
1.historical_context: The 'weak' and 'strong' controversy in chs.14–15 almost certainly reflects the Jew-Gentile tensions in the Roman church that have underlain the entire letter. Jewish Christians observed food laws and Sabbath; Gentile Christians regarded these as no longer binding. The 'weak in faith' are those whose faith has not yet enabled them to live in the full freedom of the gospel with respect to these adiaphora (indifferent matters); the 'strong' are those who have grasped that food and days are neither inherently holy nor impure. Paul aligns himself with the strong (15:1) but argues against their contempt for the weak. The food issue may also involve meat sold after idol-sacrifice, an issue Paul addresses in 1 Corinthians 8–10 from a similar angle. The Roman church's Jew-Gentile composition makes the social dimension of this dispute acute.
1.literary_structure: The passage addresses both sides of the dispute: those who eat are warned not to despise those who abstain (v.3); those who abstain are warned not to judge those who eat (v.3, 10). The theological grounding is christological: each person is accountable to the Lord who owns them (vv.7–9), not to the community's shared preference. The divine judgment seat (v.10) relativizes all mutual human judging: when we all stand before God, our concern with others' dietary choices will appear in its proper perspective. The 'to the Lord' repetition (vv.6, 8) is the key: every act of eating or abstaining, when done with thanksgiving to God, is an act of worship directed to the Lord—and neither act can be condemned as long as it is genuinely oriented toward him.
1.theological_application: The principle of 'welcome the one who is weak in faith, but not to quarrel over opinions' (v.1) distinguishes between the central matters of the gospel (on which clarity and unity are essential) and the secondary matters of practice (on which diversity within a shared Lord-orientation is permissible). The church must make this distinction carefully: not everything is an adiaphoron, but much that is treated as essential is actually secondary, and genuine Christian communities will include people at different points in their understanding and practice. The judgment-seat perspective of verse 10 is the most powerful antidote to mutual contempt and judgment within the community: when everyone is equally accountable to the Lord who owns them, the energy invested in monitoring others' secondary practices is revealed as a misallocation of moral energy. The community's task is to welcome, not to sort—to receive those whom God has received, even when their practice differs from ours.
1.christology: 'To this end Christ died and lived again, that he might be Lord both of the dead and of the living' (v.9) is the christological ground for the entire weak-strong argument: the risen Christ is Lord of all believers—eating and abstaining, Jew and Gentile, dead and alive. His cross and resurrection established his lordship over all human existence, which means that every person in the community stands under his ownership and accountability, not under the ownership and accountability of the other members. This eliminates both the strong person's contempt (you are not this person's judge; Christ is) and the weak person's condemnation (you are not this person's master; Christ is). The community's horizontal relations are transformed when both parties refer their lives to the same vertical Lord who died for them and lives over them.

13.pericope_label: Do Not Cause Another to Stumble
13.synthesis: Therefore let us not pass judgment on one another any longer, but rather decide never to put a stumbling block or hindrance in the way of a brother (Romans 14:13). Paul knows and is persuaded in the Lord Jesus that nothing is unclean in itself, but it is unclean for anyone who thinks it unclean (Romans 14:14). If your brother is grieved by what you eat, you are no longer walking in love. By what you eat, do not destroy the one for whom Christ died (Romans 14:15). Do not let what you regard as good be spoken of as evil. For the kingdom of God is not a matter of eating and drinking but of righteousness and peace and joy in the Holy Spirit (Romans 14:16–17). Whoever serves Christ in this way is acceptable to God and approved by men. So let us pursue what makes for peace and for mutual upbuilding (Romans 14:18–19). Do not for the sake of food destroy the work of God. Everything is clean, but it is wrong to make others stumble by what you eat (Romans 14:20). It is good not to eat meat or drink wine or do anything that causes your brother to stumble (Romans 14:21). The faith that you have, keep between yourself and God. Blessed is the one who has no reason to pass judgment on himself for what he approves. But whoever has doubts is condemned if he eats, because the eating is not from faith; for whatever does not proceed from faith is sin (Romans 14:22–23).
13.historical_context: The principle that 'nothing is unclean in itself' (v.14) reflects the christological transformation of purity categories: the clean-unclean distinction of Torah has been superseded in Christ, as Peter's vision (Acts 10) and Jesus' declaration (Mark 7:19) indicated. But Paul's point is pastoral, not theological: even though the strong person is correct in principle, the exercise of that freedom becomes wrong when it becomes a stumbling block for someone whose conscience is not yet free. 'The one for whom Christ died' (v.15) is the measure of the weak person's worth: Christ thought it worthwhile to die for this person whose conscience is over-scrupulous; the strong person should think it worthwhile to restrict their freedom for the same person. 'Whatever does not proceed from faith is sin' (v.23) is a general principle about the integrity of action: acting against conscience—even when the conscience is misinformed—is sinful, because it is acting in a way one believes is wrong.
13.literary_structure: The section shifts from 'don't judge each other' (v.13a) to 'take care not to cause stumbling' (v.13b)—the responsibility shifts from both parties to the strong. The kingdom-of-God definition (v.17) provides the criterion for what matters: if the kingdom is about righteousness, peace, and joy in the Holy Spirit, then food-disputes are not kingdom-level concerns—but the destruction of a fellow believer is. The principle builds: freedom is real (nothing unclean in itself) → but love limits its exercise (don't destroy the one for whom Christ died) → because the kingdom is bigger than food → so pursue peace and mutual upbuilding → and when in doubt, don't. The chapter closes with the conscience-principle (v.23) that protects the weak person's integrity even in their mistaken scrupulosity.
13.theological_application: 'Do not destroy the work of God for the sake of food' (v.20) is the most concentrated statement of the chapter's ethics. The 'work of God' is the person whose faith is at stake—God has been building this person's faith, and the strong person's careless exercise of freedom can undo that building. The implication for the church is that freedom is always exercised in community, and community relations constrain freedom at the point where freedom becomes destructive. The kingdom-of-God definition of verse 17 is particularly important for church community discernment: when disputes center on practices rather than the essential goods of righteousness, peace, and Spirit-joy, the community has misidentified what it exists to protect. The conscience principle of verse 23 is the deepest insight: integrity of action—doing only what one can do in faith before God—is more important than the abstract correctness of any position. This protects both the weak (don't pressure them to act against conscience) and the strong (don't exercise freedom in ways that invite weak persons into conscience-violation).
13.christology: 'Do not destroy the one for whom Christ died' (v.15) is the supreme argument against using Christian freedom to harm fellow believers: Christ's death establishes the value of every community member, including the weakest and most scrupulous. The one who seems to be an obstacle to the strong person's freedom is the person Christ regarded as worth dying for—a perspective that should fundamentally reshape how the strong regard the weak. The risen Christ's lordship over both dead and living (v.9) means that he is Lord of both the strong and weak, and that both are answerable to him for how they treat each other. The servant of Christ who 'serves Christ in this way' (v.18—pursuing peace and upbuilding, restricting freedom for the sake of the weak) is not diminished but approved by God: the living sacrifice of one's freedom for another's faith is precisely the love-ethics that fulfills the law and pleases the Lord who gave himself for all.
